analogy: Think of "The Story of Indian Democracy" through the chapter's own opening images: You will realise that the Constitution has the capacity to help people because it is based on the basic norms of social justice. For instance, the Directive Principle on Village Panchayats was moved as an amendment in the Constituent Assembly by K. After forty odd... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 3.1 The PanchayaTi Raj and The challenges, 3.2 Political Parties, Pressure and interest, The Story of Indian Democracy, Story. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
The Story of Indian Democracy is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that you will realise that the Constitution has the capacity to help people because it is based on the basic norms of social justice.

The text explains that for instance, the Directive Principle on Village Panchayats was moved as an amendment in the Constituent Assembly by K.

The text explains that after forty odd years, it became a Constitutional imperative after the 73rd Amendment in 1992.

The text explains that the Constitution is not just a ready referencer of do’s and don’ts for social justice.

The text explains that it has the potential to extend the meaning of social justice.

The text explains that social movements have also aided the courts and authorities to interpret the contents of rights and principles in keeping with the contemporary understanding on social justice.

The text explains that constitutional norms and soCial JustiCe: interpretation to aid soCial JustiCe It is useful to understand that there is a difference between law and justice.

The text explains that law is law because it carries the means to coerce or force obedience.

The text explains that any system of law functions through a hierarchy of authorities.

The text explains that constitution is the basis of all rules and authorities.

The text explains that it is the document that constitutes a nation’s tenets.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution is India’s basic norm.

The text explains that all other laws are made as per the procedures prescribed by the Constitution.

The text explains that these laws are made and implemented by the authorities specified by the Constitution.

The text explains that a hierarchy of courts (which too are authorities created by the Constitution) inerpret the laws when there is a dispute.

The text explains that the Supreme Court is the highest court and the ultimate interpreter of the Constitution.

The text explains that the Supreme Court has enhanced the substance of Fundamental Rights in the Constitution in many important ways.

The text explains that the Box below illustrates a few instances.  A Fundamental Right includes all that is incidental to it.

The text explains that the terse words of Article 21 recognising the right to life and liberty have been interpreted as including all that goes into a life of quality, including livelihood, health, shelter, education and dignity.

The text explains that in various pronouncements, different attributes of ‘life’ have been expanded and ‘life’ has been explained to mean more than mere animal existence.

The text explains that in 1993 the Supreme Court held that Right to Information is a part of and incidental to the Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19(1) (a).  The Supreme Court read the Directive Principle of Equal Pay for Equal Work into the Fundamental Rights to Equality under Article 14 and has provided relief to many plantation and agricultural labourers and to others.

The text explains that while the idea of grassroot democracy is not an alien import to our country, in a society where there are sharp inequalities, democratic participation is hindered on grounds of gender, caste and class.

The text explains that furthermore, as you shall see in the newspaper reports later in the chapter, traditionally there have been caste panchayats in villages.

The text explains that when the constitution was being drafted, panchayats did not find a mention in it.

The text explains that ambedkar argued that local elites and upper castes were so well entrenched in society that local self- government only meant a continuing exploitation of the downtrodden masses of Indian society.

The text explains that the concept of local government was dear to Gandhiji too.

The text explains that it was, however, only in 1992 that grassroot democracy or decentralised governance was ushered in by the 73rd Constitutional Amendment.

The text explains that this act provided constitutional status to the Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs).

The text explains that it is compulsory now for local self-government bodies in rural and municipal areas to be elected every five years.

The text explains that more importantly, control of local resources is given to the elected local bodies.

The text explains that 33 The three-tier system of Panchayati Raj Institution  The structure is like a pyramid.

The text explains that at the base of the structure stands the unit of democracy or Gram Sabha.

The text explains that it is this general body that elects the local government and charges it with specific responsibilities.

The text explains that the Gram Sabhas ideally ought to provide an open forum for discussions and village-level development activities and play a crucial role in ensuring inclusion of the weaker sections in the decision-making processes.  The 73rd Amendment provided a three-tier system of Panchayati Raj for all states having a population of over twenty lakhs.  It became mandatory that election to these bodies be conducted every five years.  It provided reservation of seats for the Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes and thirty three percent seats for women.  It constituted District Planning Committee to prepare drafts and develop plans for the district as a whole.

The text explains that box 3.2 The Constitution and Social Change 33

The text explains that the 73rd and 74th amendments to the Constitution ensured the reservation of one third of the total seats for women in all elected offices of local bodies in both the rural and urban areas.

The text explains that the 1993–94 elections, soon after the 73rd amendment brought in 800,000 women into the political processes in a single election.

The text explains that a constitutional amendment prescribed a three- tier system of local self-governance (read Box 3.7 on the last page) for the entire country, effective since 1992–93. powers and responsibilities of panChayats According to the Constitution, Panchayats should be given powers and authority to function as institutions of self-government.

The text explains that it, thus, requires all state governments to revitalise local representative institutions.

The text explains that if not, find out and write a note about them in about 200 words.

The text explains that the 73rd amendment has been monumental in bringing a voice to the people in the villages.

The text explains that write an essay on the ways that the Indian Constitution touches peoples’ everyday life, drawing upon different examples.

The text explains that what is the significance of political parties in a democracy?

The text explains that what is the role of interest groups in a democratic system?

The text explains that questions The Constitution and Social Change 39

The text explains that 2006. ‘Disconnecting Experience: Making World Class Roads in Mumbai’.

The text explains that economic and Political Weekly (August 5th). pp.

The text explains that the Argumentative Indian: Writings on Indian History, Culture and Identity.

The text explains that essays in Sociology Ed. with an introduction by H.H.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 3: The Story of Indian Democracy

prelims_traps: Do not treat 3.1 The PanchayaTi Raj and The challenges and 3.2 Political Parties, Pressure and interest as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Story of Indian Democracy; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Story of Indian Democracy through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 3.1 The PanchayaTi Raj and The challenges and 3.2 Political Parties, Pressure and interest to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
